original_abstract:
Anthropogenic climate change is driving significant and non-uniform alterations to the global water cycle, a trend that has become increasingly detectable since the mid-20th century. This assessment synthesizes evidence from paleoclimate reconstructions, observational datasets, and advanced model simulations to evaluate the physical drivers and future trajectories of these changes. There is high confidence that global mean precipitation and evaporation increase with surface warming—estimated at a rate of 1–3% per degree Celsius—governed by Earth’s energy budget and thermodynamic constraints. However, regional outcomes are heavily modified by atmospheric circulation shifts, such as a slowdown of tropical overturning and a poleward migration of storm tracks.

Since the late 20th century, human influence has contributed to an overall moistening of the atmosphere and an intensification of heavy precipitation events, while simultaneously exacerbating aridity and drought severity in regions such as the Mediterranean, southern Africa, and western North America. Anthropogenic aerosols have further complicated these patterns, having historically suppressed global precipitation and driven significant shifts in tropical rain belts, notably contributing to the Sahelian droughts of the 1970s and 1980s. Future projections indicate that without substantial emissions reductions, the contrast between wet and dry seasons and regimes will sharpen. Annual precipitation is projected to increase in high latitudes and equatorial regions, while robust drying is expected in the subtropics. Furthermore, water cycle extremes and interannual variability are expected to increase faster than mean changes, manifesting in more frequent and severe floods and droughts. While improved model resolution has enhanced the representation of regional processes, significant uncertainties remain regarding internal variability and the potential for abrupt transitions, such as those associated with an Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation collapse or Amazonian deforestation tipping points. Under continuous warming, the global water cycle will increasingly be dominated by greenhouse gas forcing, leading to profound impacts on freshwater availability and ecosystem stability.
humanized_abstract:
Anthropogenic climate change is affecting the global water cycle in many substantial and geo-graphically disparate ways, and these changes are detectable and becoming more apparent with time—a phenomenon that began in the mid-20th century. Observational, paleoclimate, and modelling evidence synthesised in this assessment helps us to understand better what may be causing these changes and what are likely to be their consequences. With very high confidence, a variety of lines of evidence suggest that components of the global water cycle—global mean precipitation and evaporation—are increasing in response to rising atmospheric temperatures at rates on the order of a few percent per degree global warming, largely as a consequence of a change in the global energy balance and thermodynamic relations of the Earth system. However, local responses have been profoundly modified by large-scale changes in atmospheric circulation patterns that are also part of the global water cycle response, such as weakening of tropical atmospheric convective thermodinaric circulation cells and shifts in the position of mid-latitude weather systems.

On Earth, since late 20th century, human influence has led to a generally wetter atmosphere, which is accompanied with an increase in very heavy precipitation events. However, at the same time, strong regional drying occurs with varying spatial extent (such as over the Mediterranean, southern Africa and parts of western North America). Additionally, aerosols increase precipitation, in part, by shifting tropical rain belts and have caused the Sahelian droughts of the 1970s and 1980s. The wet and dry differences between seasons and between regimes will strengthen unless strong emission reductions are implemented. An increase in annual average precipitation in the high latitudes and the equatorial region, along with robust drying in the sub-tropics are projected. Exremes and interannual variability of the water cycle will change at a higher rate than the mean value, with the implication of more frequent and severe floods and droughts. Better modeling of regional processes and improvements from model resolution increases the confidence in the model projections but internal climate variability is not yet fully quantified and model uncertainties with respect to possible abrupt changes such as a collapse of the AMOC or a tipping point for the Amazon rainforest remain. These changes will strongly influence the Earth climate system as aerosol induced effects are gradually replaced by greenhouse gas induced effects and many changes are catastrophic to freshwater resources and ecosystems.
